description:
You are acting as a database administrator who has been asked to start refactoring query files by getting rid of the indiscriminate “SELECT *” pattern.

Directory layout already present in the container:

/home/user/db  
├── queries  
│   ├── daily  
│   │   └── report.sql  
│   ├── archive  
│   │   └── old_report.sql  
│   └── maintenance  
│       └── cleanup.sql  
└── optimization  
    └── (empty)

Important facts about the starting files (you can verify with cat, wc, etc.)  
1. /home/user/db/queries/daily/report.sql – 3 lines  
   /* daily report */  
   SELECT * FROM sales;  
   LIMIT 100;  

2. /home/user/db/queries/archive/old_report.sql – 2 lines  
   /* archived report */  
   SELECT * FROM sales;  

3. /home/user/db/queries/maintenance/cleanup.sql – 2 lines  
   /* maintenance task */  
   DELETE FROM logs WHERE date < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days';  

Timestamps have been set so that **only report.sql has a modification date within the last 7 days**; the other two files are older.

Your job  
1. Locate every “.sql” file **inside /home/user/db/queries/** (recursively) that  
   • was modified in the last 7 days and  
   • contains the exact string “SELECT *” (with no extra spaces).  
2. In each qualifying file, replace every occurrence of  
   SELECT *  
   with the safer stub  
   SELECT id, name  
   (preserve case; do NOT add a semicolon).  
3. For every file you actually changed, append one line to a log file at  
   /home/user/db/optimization/optimization.log  

   The required log-line format (single-line, no spaces other than those already inside the file path):  
   relative/path/from_/home/user/db|ORIGINAL_LINE_COUNT|NEW_LINE_COUNT  

   Example (do not literally include this; it is just a sample format):  
   queries/daily/example.sql|15|15  

4. The log file must:  
   • contain one entry per modified file, no duplicates;  
   • be sorted alphabetically by the relative path;  
   • end with a trailing newline.  

Constraints & hints  
• Accomplish the entire task using at most 3–4 Shell commands (think find … | xargs …).  
• Do NOT hard-code file names; use find, grep, xargs, wc, etc.  
• The replacement must be in-place—no new temporary *.sql files should remain.  
• Create /home/user/db/optimization/optimization.log if it does not already exist; subsequent runs should append new lines without rewriting existing ones.  
• The automated checker will verify:  
   – “SELECT *” no longer appears in eligible files;  
   – unaffected files remain untouched;  
   – optimization.log exists with exactly the expected single line and correct counts and format.

Whenever you need to confirm progress, feel free to print console output, but remember that the final state of the filesystem is what will be tested.
truth:
Initial file contents:

/home/user/db/queries/daily/report.sql:
Line1: /* daily report */
Line2: SELECT * FROM sales;
Line3: LIMIT 100;

/home/user/db/queries/archive/old_report.sql:
Line1: /* archived report */
Line2: SELECT * FROM sales;

/home/user/db/queries/maintenance/cleanup.sql:
Line1: /* maintenance task */
Line2: DELETE FROM logs WHERE date < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days';

Expected final state after correct execution:

1. /home/user/db/queries/daily/report.sql contents:
Line1: /* daily report */
Line2: SELECT id, name FROM sales;
Line3: LIMIT 100;

   (Note: only the string “SELECT *” changed to “SELECT id, name”; spacing outside the asterisk remains untouched.)

2. /home/user/db/queries/archive/old_report.sql and /home/user/db/queries/maintenance/cleanup.sql should remain **unchanged** compared with initial state.

3. /home/user/db/optimization/optimization.log must exist and contain exactly:
queries/daily/report.sql|3|3\n
(that is: the string “queries/daily/report.sql|3|3” followed by a single newline; nothing else)

Permissions: all files live under /home/user where the agent has write access.

The grading script will:
• grep -R "SELECT \*" /home/user/db/queries and expect no matches under daily/report.sql
• diff unchanged files to ensure they are identical to initial versions
• cat /home/user/db/optimization/optimization.log and compare exactly to the line listed above
